reviews.review_text:
The misunderstood middle child of the franchise where everyone is Australian and gravity is entirely optional.

Borderlands: The Pre-Sequel is the classic definition of an underrated gem. Stuck directly in the massive shadow of Borderlands 2, it often gets unfairly brushed aside by the community. However, if you actually give it a chance, you'll find a fantastic looter-shooter that takes everything great about its predecessor and shoots it directly to the moon. Literally. Set on the lunar surface of Elpis, the game leans heavily into its development by 2K Australia. The result is an incredibly fun, unhinged world packed with fantastic Aussie humor, bizarre slang, and space bogans trying to murder you in a vacuum.

The biggest shift here is the combat loop, which arguably features some of the most fun mobility in the entire franchise. The introduction of low gravity and "Oz" (oxygen) kits completely changes how you approach a firefight. You aren't just hiding behind cover anymore; you are soaring fifty feet into the air, raining down newly introduced laser fire, and executing devastating "butt slams" to crush enemies beneath you. Furthermore, Cryo (ice) weapons replace the tedious Slag mechanic from the second game. There is absolutely nothing quite as satisfying as freezing a leaping psycho mid-air and shattering him into a million icy pieces with a shotgun blast. It adds a layer of verticality that makes going back to ground-bound movement feel sluggish.

From a narrative standpoint, it is an absolute must-play for fans of the lore. Getting a front-row seat to watch the tragic, slow-burn corruption of Handsome Jack—watching him devolve from a slightly arrogant but well-meaning hero into the greatest villain in gaming history—is a fantastic ride. Plus, the Vault Hunters themselves have incredibly deep, well-designed skill trees that rival anything in the main games. Where else in the gaming industry are you actively encouraged to play as Claptrap, whose ultimate ability might randomly force your entire co-op team to start bouncing around like rubber balls?

8/10 — I came for the loot, I stayed to weaponize Claptrap's existential dread.

Borderlands: The Pre-Sequel feels like a mix between the first 2 games, but with its own weird moon-themed shenanigans. The biggest difference is the low-gravity gameplay, which lets you jump huge distances, slam into enemies, and move around fights much faster. It makes combat feel more vertical and chaotic, especially once you start using laser and cryo weapons.

The atmosphere is also surprisingly strong. Elpis, the moon of Pandora, feels cold, empty, and isolated in a way that sometimes brings back the lonely sci-fi western vibe of the first game. The soundtrack helps a lot too, with ambient electronic music that fits the space setting really well. Compared to Borderlands 2, the tone feels a little quieter and less overwhelming. 

One of the best parts of the game is seeing Handsome Jack before he fully becomes the villain everyone knows. Instead of already being completely insane, you slowly watch him become more ruthless and unstable over the course of the story. That makes him feel more human and, honestly, a bit tragic at times, even if he is clearly heading in a bad direction.

The playable characters are also interesting, as you've met all of them before: Athena appeared in the first game, in a DLC fighting the Atlas Corporation; the ever-present Claptrap has appeared in all the games and is as annoying (or charming) as ever; and Wilhelm and Nisha are both bosses in BL2. There are also 2 DLC characters, both of whom will appear in BL3: Jack the Doppelganger and Lady Aurelia Hammerlock (the sister of the posh, Victorian-era hunter, Sir Hammerlock). The hero characters are still fun and memorable, each with their own quips and quotes, and fit perfectly with the strange world of the game.

The game still has some problems, though. A lot of areas can feel repetitive, the oxygen system is more annoying than interesting, and overall it does not feel as big or polished as Borderlands 2. Even so, The Pre-Sequel is underrated in a lot of ways. It may not reach the highs of Borderlands 2, but the movement, atmosphere, and character builds make it a really fun game for Borderlands fans, even if at times it feels more of the same.

would be better than bl2 if it had an endgame outside of 1 dlc and a slightly buffed version of the final boss as a raid boss
also the games story got people trying to reason with space hitler
10/10

This game feels like a clearly improved version of the first game. The dialogue is better, the characters talk more, the level design is miles ahead, and there are a bunch of new mechanics that make the whole thing feel less dry. The loot also feels better compared to the first game, and the bosses are generally more enjoyable too.

But — and it is a pretty big but — the mission design still can’t really escape the same problem. It’s still mostly “go kill this many enemies” or “go grab this thing and bring it back,” which was already my biggest issue with the first Borderlands. The better dialogue and stronger atmosphere make it less painful here, but the core problem is still sitting there.

The grind also feels heavier this time, and until I unlocked some actually useful skills, the combat could get genuinely rough. So yeah, it improves a lot of things from the first game, but it still carries enough of the same boring mission structure to hold it back.

6/10 - A little bit above average.

This Borderlands is honestly my least favorite (I haven't played BL4 yet) However It is still a very interesting sequel prequel with many good things about it, especially with the experimental zero gravity effects and oxygen.
So Far the store is great and so is the game mechanics.

Okay so there's a lot of problems with this game sadly. The pacing, the writing (at times), the level design, the voice acting, performance (looking at you PhysX) and the lack of content. There's good parts and it shines above BL2 sometimes, so I'd still recommend it, but it's hard to ignore the wasted potential. The main reason for this is Australia 2K shutting down and the near-abandonment by Gearbox.

This game could have been really good, and I think this is most apparent in the Claptastic voyage DLC, it felt like a significant improvement over the base game and it's a shame BLTPS couldn't get more development time.

I'd still recommend this game, but it's replayability feels considerably more stunted than BL2, TVHM (2nd playthrough) feels like a slog that I wouldn't recommend.

I'm late to the BL party, but having 'binged' BL2, PS, and BL3 last year, this is the one that stands out the most to me. Not just the fun low gravity gameplay, but the story that gave you a compelling reason to empathize with Jack, and a layered and strong character like Athena. (I played BL3 with Amara who is like the personification of 'toxic masculinity')
My biggest NOTE!!!! is that they need to allow you to re-play with pre-leveled characters! I would replay the game several more times now that I'm nearing my end of my TVHM repaly, but I'm not interested in starting from zero. Let me try out a new character, now that I've beaten the game (twice)!
Oh, and I LOVE all the Australian talent. As a lifelong skeptic, Australia (despite their 'Fox-News-But-Somehow-More-Racist' population) has a special place in my heart and mind! <3

Borderlands The Pre Sequel is a good game with some great ideas. The low gravity movement makes combat more dynamic and fun, and the new laser weapons add variety to the usual Borderlands arsenal. The Vault Hunters are also excellent, with strong build diversity and some of the best Vault Hunters in the franchise. The story, especially around Handsome Jack, is engaging and adds a lot to the overall universe. However, the game is held back by noticeable flaws. Many maps feel empty, repetitive, and visually dull, and even the moon setting starts to lose its impact over time. Level progression and side quests are poorly paced, often forcing unnecessary backtracking through uninteresting areas. The Claptrap DLC is often praised, but for me it was one of the worst DLCs with bullet sponge enemies and it feels more messy than memorable. Endgame content is also very limited, which makes the later stages feel repetitive and reduces replay value. Overall, it’s still a solid Borderlands experience with great mechanics and strong characters, but its weak map design, pacing issues, and lack of endgame content stop it from being truly great. It’s the weakest main entry for me, and the only Borderlands game I’ve never playing again. 6/10

It was a better game when I had unlimited golden keys that reappeared after I used them up more than 5o plus at a time. When that went away I was bummed. But I am having more fun learning the game again just using what I can find, earn, or buy. The grinder is mostly a disappointment and way too weird to spend time figuring out. Have a good time it is always challenging and keeps you busy. I really hated blands 3. just too much bs and maps and space ship baloney. The original and bland 2 are excellent too and replayable too.

So this game won't start until I rip out my internet connection. It can't get past firewall so I have to wait an infinite amount of time? Every time. Yeah, no.

But it's still borderlands, and it's still good.

Borderlands: The Pre-Sequel sits in a strange middle ground for the series. It keeps the trademark Borderlands humor and chaotic gunplay, but leans more into experimentation than into a tight, satisfying gameplay loop. The low‑gravity movement and jetpacks feel fresh at first, yet the novelty fades because the game doesn’t offer enough variety to support its length. The weapons are fun, the tone is familiar, and the moon setting has its charm, but the quests often drag on longer than they should. There is a lot of talking—excessive dialogue, constant voice lines, and long exchanges that slow down the pacing when all you want is to shoot things and grab loot.

If you enjoy the Borderlands universe and want something lighter and more chaotic, The Pre-Sequel delivers a decent experience, just not on the same level as the mainline entries.

Thought the game was pretty good had a decent ending. Not the best of the borderlands franchise but enjoyable enough. only part i didnt care for seemed like drops were stale didnt feel like i got many legendarys, while on my 100% completion playthrough.

if you love the franchise this is a great story to play through once, and the Claptastic dlc is one of the best in the entire franchise. BUT its a grinding nightmare, it takes 100's of hours of BS scarcity-driven grinding to get any good gear even after three playthroughs - the devs just don't reward your effort and it feels like a punishing grind, so unless you love the franchise go do something else.

in my personal opinion, best game in the series. story is nice, actually balanced ng+ rather than bl2's mess of systems, best dlc in the series (shock drop slaughter pit obviously lmao), awesome spacey art direction. add some python sdk mods and you could replay this game autistically for a looongg time

Dont get me wrong, its a great game and I played a ♥♥♥♥ ton on the switch and I love the space theme. But this game has the same problem as borderlands 2.. The "out of video memory" crash. Nothing helped and I just give up. Waste of my damn money. 

Hope borderlands 3 does not have it..
Shorter but gud. Jumpy mechanics is gud.

I recommend it, BUT:

1) I installed it after playing BL3. Compared to that the gameplay feel very clunky
2) Ground vehicle steering with only mouse aiming is VERY unintuitive. The moon buggy stops at everything protruding from the ground a little.
3) Low gravity SLOWS the action - it takes forever to get close to the enemies. Forget using shotgun. Sniper rifle rules them all. 
4) No sliding
5) Story is maybe a little more interesting but nothing to be amazed by... BL series was never about story. It is about action. And the action pace here is like turn based strategy game.
6) Weapons have no such variety like in BL3. GOLD and PURPLE weapons are very hard to come by.
7) No automatic items pickup. U have to press E for a long time to pick up everything around U.
8) No fast travel from anywhere on the map. U have to go to the right fast travel station. There is only one on each location.
9) It is faster to Quit the game to the main menu and RESUME than jump in zero gravity back to the start of the map
10) VERY NICE usage of PhySX NVIDIA physics of debris. So, If you have RTX 4k or older U will get very nice effects. Sorry 5k users :(.
11) Game freezes from time to time :/

It's one of the worst Borderlands in the series however Playing it for the second time After release, I really enjoyed it. I don't understand why. it gets so much hate but after playing it the second time my initial thoughts changed And I would recommend Giving it another shot Or playing it for the first time. 8/10
Weak start but good game, there is some live service ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ around and they lock forward progression behind numbers, forcing you to do some side quests for XP, but still enjoyable.

Borderlands The Pre Sequel is a lot like the uncle you keep hearing about. You know the one. He cut off your family and lives a healthy, active and unique lifestyle, which your family keeps describing as boring or "the worst one in the family".

The truth is that this is most likely overall, the most well rounded game in the series and it could have been even better than 2 if the studio was given time to finish all the DLC and content. The content that we did receive is, undoubtedly in my opinion the single best DLC in Borderlands history, the best story in the series and absolutely the best vault hunter lineup in the series and the rest dont even come close. 

This is really the only borderlands game ive played where I havent felt like one or two of the characters are bad. Yes, there is Wilhelm, but he isnt bad. The lineup is just so busted this time that hes the most normal out of all of them, and thats very usual for the introductory soldier class in all of the games. All of the characters are extremely fun to use and viable in the end game. And theyre all incredibly distinct and unique.

The game introduces new mechanics that were quite overlooked or viewed negatively on release but now I think are viewed normally. New melee mechanics such as the slam, new weapons class such as the laser, the grinder which allows you to combine weapons and receive higher rarity weapons including legendaries, and the Zero G environment, which adds very fun and speedy movement using the oxygen kits that you find. And all of these are very fun features and they really felt fun after BL2.

The DLC is the best in the series, alongside the story.  The story is never anything to boast about in any of the borderlands games, but this time...magically, everything is fine. Funny jokes, funny characters with humor that isnt always screaming in your face, and the most character development in the series - all of your vault hunters have unique dialogue associated with them, and they are referred to with their names and not "VH" or "Killer". Overall, I felt quite happy with all the characters and was intrigued by them. The DLC is funny, fun to play, and very unique with the best boss fight in the series in my opinion. This is the only time that Claptrap is given semi serious character development and backstory, which i appreciate.

Theres also some unique mechanics that dont add much, like oxygen and atmosphere. You can only light enemies on fire if they are in atmosphere, which you can trigger by activating an oxygen generator or going inside the building. Theres also the new Cryo element which is fun to use.

Overall, I think this is a great game. The only qualm is its rather short, has only one raid boss, and the baseline bosses just arent that memorable apart from one or two. The game was unfortunately cut down before it could reach its prime, but it could have been the best. I love you pre sequel. im sorry others didnt.

Men are born for games. Every child knows that play is nobler than work. And Borderlands The Pre-Sequel is a game that should be played, despite what some might say.
worth playing, community patch makes it much better.

While the Borderland Pre-Sequel adds a lot of cool new concepts, it ultimately feels like a step down from Borderlands 2.  On the positive side, I enjoyed the new character skill trees, the introduction of laser weapons, and the low-gravity gameplay (kind of reminded me of Destiny's Warlock jump).  On the downside, the game feels like it has a lot less content, less endgame bosses, and just has a lot of collision bugs.  I had fun with it, but this will probably be the last Borderlands game I play.

Listen, I LOVE the Borderlands games. I haven't played 4 yet, but I know I'm gonna have at least a fun time, if not a long time. My favourite is Wonderlands, but I adore all of them equally really. So it really hurts me to write this negative review. And here's the reason why it's a negative:

The game's broken.

Now, jank from an older Borderlands game is to be expected. They're old and they might have some jank when it comes to running on a more modern system. But for the Pre-Sequel, there's a lot of problems just at the start of the game. For context, I played up to the same section with both Athena and Claptrap, so it's not a character problem. First red flag I saw was in the very first level, Helios Station. When you bash the loader out of the elevator after fighting the fire dude, it heads down and is SUPPOSED to let Jack get on. The problem is that the loader has a weird hitbox that spawns on it for some reason, and that hitbox pushes you AND JACK backwards. You can get on real easy, just jump. Jack won't jump. He'll dance in front of the loader until the end of time. The fix is easy but annoying. You need to exit to the menu, then reload. Jack will have teleported to the top of the elevator, ready for the moonshot. But YOU will have been respawned ALLLLLLLLLLL the way back to the very first room where you arrive when you start the game. Enemies will not respawn, so it's a boring run alllllll the way back. The next two bits of jank I saw were during the inspirational poster quest. In the first part, you use a jump pad to do a few actions. The one where you need to shoot targets is broken, the jump pad will trigger but not launch you, making you shoot the targets from the ground. Not really a gamebreaking bug, but a little strange. In the second part, where you're delivering posters, killing the dude you give the posters to will cause them to explode out and one of mine got stuck on wonky geometry. It took me 30 minutes of trying to parkour before trying for another 2 minutes to find the exact crack in the geometry it fell in cause it was in the floor. The final bit of bugginess I had was when you get a car for the first time. It's a simple tutorial: drive a little, then there's a ramp you need to boost to successfully jump from. Simple enough. The boost doesn'T work. The cars are incredibly front heavy for some reason, so you quickly nose dive no matter what speed you take the jump from. I tried ditching the car, no luck the terrain has broken collision. So while I wanted to experience this game again, years later of playing it originally, it seems I just cannot.

If you think you can handle this jank and aren't afraid to go at it, give this game a go it's such a fun time.
The last good Borderlands game, play the DLC "Claptastic Voyage" after the campaign, like all claptrap DLC, its very funny with witty humour and some dark jokes thrown in.
If you like BL2 this is worth a playthrough. The final boss is terrible and ♥♥♥♥ those eternal dahl guys.
